lens.included_activities: Lower-middle-market firms leasing passenger cars, light-duty passenger trucks, passenger vans, and sport utility vehicles without drivers for generally long periods, including recurring fleet-leasing and related account-management services sold to external customers.
lens.excluded_activities: Short-term passenger-car rental classified in 532111; chauffeured transportation; vehicle sales; captive internal fleet units; finance-only special-purpose vehicles; and firms outside the approximate $1-10M normalized EBITDA band.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Operators earn recurring lease payments over multi-month or multi-year contracts and may also charge or embed fleet-management, maintenance, telematics, compliance, fuel, and vehicle-disposal services. Customers include businesses, governments, organizations, and some individual lessees.

a.rationale: The closest published OEWS industry group shows substantial counter/rental-clerk and customer-service employment alongside vehicle cleaners, mechanics, drivers, supervisors, and managers. Long-term leasing adds document intake, quoting, account reporting, billing, maintenance authorization, claims triage, compliance, and remarketing analysis that are partly exposed to AI, while physical service, inspections, relationship management, and accountable approvals remain. The range is a task-level judgment, not an observed exposure measure.
a.change_evidence: A six-digit payroll-by-occupation extract combined with time-and-motion studies from passenger-car lessors covering quoting, credit intake, contract administration, maintenance authorizations, claims, billing, client support, and remarketing would materially refine the wage-weighted exposed share.
caveats: OEWS is published for 532100, not 532112, and reports employment rather than task time or AI exposure.
Enterprise Fleet Management is a large-provider example whose administrative technology and service mix may not represent LMM firms.
The frozen labor-share input uses 2024 wages over 2022 receipts and is harmonized to the IPS basis; it was not re-estimated.

rho.rationale: Connected-vehicle data, telematics, dashboards, cost tracking, alerts, and document storage provide structured inputs for workflow automation and AI assistance. Five-year realization is limited by legacy system integration, data rights, vehicle and contract exceptions, credit and residual-value controls, customer-specific policies, and human approval for consequential decisions.
rho.change_evidence: Vendor-verified deployment cohorts from LMM passenger-car lessors showing baseline and post-deployment handling time, straight-through processing, exception rates, error rates, overrides, staffing, and realized hours released would materially move rho.
caveats: Published technology features demonstrate digital readiness, not causal labor savings from AI.
Implementation capacity is likely lower at small regional lessors than at the cited large fleet manager.

e.rationale: The NAICS definition is inherently an external leasing activity delivered over long periods, so many operating LMM firms fit the recurring-service lens. The range excludes captive internal fleets, finance-only vehicles, non-control portfolios, and establishments whose economics are primarily vehicle sales rather than an operating lease service.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level census matching 532112 establishments to customer contracts, service revenue, ownership/control, fleet assets, and normalized EBITDA would identify the eligible share directly.
caveats: Eligibility is not measured by the NAICS definition alone.
The frozen LMM count is an ESTIMATE derived with a 30% margin assumption and may misclassify asset-heavy lessors.

s5.rationale: Equipment-rental businesses do transact, but the public marketplace data do not provide a 532112 denominator or distinguish qualifying control transfers from asset sales. The bounded five-year judgment allows for owner succession and strategic consolidation while recognizing that fleet financing, residual-value exposure, and lender consents can impede transfers.
s5.change_evidence: Five years of closed 532112 control transactions from PitchBook, Capital IQ, Axial, BizBuySell broker records, and lender files, divided by a verified annual eligible-firm population and deduplicated for repeat sales, would materially change s5.
caveats: BizBuySell reported 31 equipment-rental-and-dealer sales in 2025, but that category is broader than 532112 and its median cash flow is below the screened EBITDA band.
The estimate excludes fleet-asset sales, lease-book financings, and internal reorganizations that do not transfer an operating company.

q.rationale: Multi-period lease payments and bundled fleet services can preserve labor benefits during an existing contract, especially when service levels are fixed. At renewal, competitive bids, open-book fleet programs, gainsharing, lower administration fees, and customer demands can pass savings through; technology and integration costs also absorb part of the gross benefit.
q.change_evidence: A sample of LMM lease contracts and renewals showing fee structure, indexation, open-book provisions, gainsharing, service-level credits, repricing, technology spend, and realized margin after automation would materially refine q.
caveats: The cited provider describes service scope but does not disclose retained automation economics.
Residual-value gains or losses are asset economics and are not treated as retained AI labor benefit.

d5.bridge: Enterprise Fleet Management reported that its U.S.-and-Canada leased fleet exceeded 720,000 vehicles in FY2024 and grew 8% year over year. This is translated into a much lower roughly 3% annual industry quantity assumption for the base case, yielding about 1.15 over five years. The large-provider, two-country observation likely overstates U.S. LMM industry growth because it can include market-share gains, so the range includes flat-to-declining demand.
d5.rationale: Organizations continue to outsource vehicle access and fleet administration, while capital costs, electrification, telematics, and compliance complexity can support leasing. Remote work, reimbursement alternatives, vehicle supply cycles, and customer insourcing constrain growth, so the one-year large-company observation is heavily discounted.
d5.change_evidence: Census Service Annual Survey or Economic Census five-year constant-price receipts and leased-vehicle counts for 532112, split by firm size and adjusted for mix, would replace the company proxy.
caveats: The source covers one provider in the United States and Canada, not the U.S. LMM industry.
Fleet growth can reflect provider share gains and vehicle prices rather than constant-price industry service quantity.

o.rationale: Digital portals can shift routine reporting and service requests to self-service, but the leased vehicle, funding, title, maintenance network, safety/compliance coordination, claims handling, and end-of-term disposition still require an accountable lessor or fleet operator. Software is more likely to change the operating interface than eliminate the operator.
o.change_evidence: Buyer-level renewal and cancellation cohorts separating migrations to software-only, self-managed, direct-finance, and full-service lease models, together with contract-level service volumes, would materially move o.
caveats: The operator-required share is not directly reported.
Large customers may unbundle financing from fleet administration more readily than small customers.

narrative.executive_view: Passenger-car leasing is a recurring contractual service with a meaningful administrative layer around vehicle finance, maintenance, compliance, reporting, and disposition. AI can compress that layer, but asset funding, residual values, physical service, and customer accountability remain central and limit a purely software-led model.
narrative.ai_impact: AI can assist quote and document preparation, contract extraction, maintenance and claims triage, customer communications, invoice reconciliation, compliance monitoring, fleet recommendations, and remarketing analysis. Human review remains important for credit, exceptions, safety, customer negotiation, and asset decisions.
narrative.value_capture: Long-duration contracts can protect savings before renewal, but competitive tenders and open-book fleet programs can share benefits with customers. Retention therefore depends on contract structure and disciplined separation of labor savings from fleet depreciation and residual-value volatility.
narrative.firm_availability: The code's operating model generally fits the external recurring-service lens, although captive and finance-only entities must be screened out. Public transaction data show equipment-rental deals exist but do not support an industry-specific transfer probability.
narrative.demand_durability: Business and government fleets continue to need vehicles and lifecycle administration. Leasing can benefit from capital intensity and fleet complexity, while reimbursement, direct ownership, remote work, and customer insourcing create downside.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: The main uncertainties are the asset-heavy frozen firm-count estimate, lack of six-digit task data, concentration among large fleet managers, residual-value cycles, vehicle financing access, and the possibility that savings are competed away at renewal.
narrative.principal_driver: Recurring multi-period contracts and data-rich fleet administration create a practical base for AI-enabled workflow compression.
narrative.principal_weakness: Asset economics, lender constraints, and large-provider concentration can dominate the labor-saving thesis and make the estimated LMM population unreliable.

sources.title: 2022 NAICS Definition: 532112 Passenger Car Leasing
sources.supports: Defines 532112 as establishments leasing passenger cars without drivers, generally for long periods, and includes passenger cars, light-duty passenger trucks, passenger vans, and SUVs.

sources.title: BLS OEWS: Largest Occupations in Automotive Equipment Rental and Leasing, May 2024
sources.supports: Reports the largest occupations for NAICS 532100, including 62,200 counter and rental clerks, 23,960 vehicle/equipment cleaners, 12,300 bus and truck mechanics, 10,840 heavy truck drivers, 7,080 general and operations managers, and 5,790 customer service representatives.

sources.title: Enterprise Fleet Management: Fleet Management Services, Tracking, and Vehicle Leasing
sources.supports: Describes fleet leasing and management supported by analytics, client strategy managers, telematics, mobile tools, dashboards, real-time vehicle and cost tracking, roadside assistance, compliance, alerts, and document and claims storage.

sources.title: Enterprise Mobility FY2024 Growth Report
sources.supports: Reports approximately 900,000 vehicles under management in the United States and Canada and a leased fleet above 720,000 vehicles, up 8% year over year.

sources.title: BizBuySell Insight Report Data Tables: Full-Year 2025 Transactions
sources.supports: Reports 31 closed transactions in the broad Equipment Rental and Dealers category during 2025, with median cash flow of $273,854, demonstrating deal activity but not an industry-specific transfer rate.
